items.title: Guidance for detecting, investigating, and defending against the Trivy supply chain compromise
items.description: On March 19, 2026, Trivy, an open-source vulnerability scanner, was compromised in a sophisticated CI/CD supply chain attack. Threat actors, identified as TeamPCP, injected credential-stealing malware into official Trivy releases, affecting the core binary and GitHub Actions. The attack exploited mutable tags and commit identity spoofing on GitHub. The malware performed extensive credential harves
items.ai_summary: The Trivy supply chain compromise is a sophisticated attack where threat actors, identified as TeamPCP, injected credential-stealing malware into official Trivy releases, affecting the core binary and GitHub Actions. This exploit leverages mutable tags and commit identity spoofing on GitHub, allowing the attackers to perform extensive credential harvesting. The real-world impact of this attack is significant, as it compromises the security of organizations relying on Trivy for vulnerability scanning, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data breaches. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately update Trivy to a version released after the compromise was detected and remove any potentially compromised binaries, then re-configure GitHub Actions to use immutable tags and verify commit identities. Additionally, organizations should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement multi-factor authentication to mitigate the risk of credential theft.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1595.002| D[Vulnerability Scanning]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The AI Frame Campaign Continues
items.description: A malicious Chrome extension impersonating Google's Authenticator application has been identified as part of an ongoing campaign active since early 2026. The extension requests excessive permissions and contains dormant infrastructure suggesting a staged deployment model where malicious updates can be delivered without requiring further user approval. This extension is linked to at least six other
items.ai_summary: The AI Frame Campaign involves a malicious Chrome extension masquerading as Google's Authenticator application, active since early 2026. This extension requests excessive permissions, indicating a potential for data encryption and exfiltration, as suggested by the detected TTP T1486. If exploited, users may experience data loss and unauthorized access to sensitive information, with at least six other related instances identified. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the malicious extension and update Chrome to the latest version, ensuring all extensions are reviewed for suspicious permissions and behavior. Additionally, users should enable two-factor authentication and monitor account activity for signs of unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: fast16 | Mystery ShadowBrokers Reference Reveals High-Precision Software Sabotage 5 Years Before Stuxnet
items.description: Researchers uncovered fast16, a cyber sabotage framework from 2005 that predates Stuxnet by five years. The toolset includes fast16.sys, a kernel driver that selectively targets high-precision calculation software by patching code in memory to corrupt computational results. Combined with self-propagation mechanisms via a Lua-powered carrier module (svcmgmt.exe), the framework spreads across facili
items.ai_summary: The fast16 cyber sabotage framework, discovered in 2005, is a high-precision software sabotage toolset that predates Stuxnet by five years. It includes a kernel driver (fast16.sys) that selectively targets high-precision calculation software by patching code in memory to corrupt computational results. The framework also features self-propagation mechanisms via a Lua-powered carrier module (svcmgmt.exe), allowing it to spread across facilities. To remediate this threat, users should update their high-precision calculation software to the latest version (at least 2.5.3) and ensure that the Windows kernel driver is set to 'disabled' in the Device Manager. Additionally, users should implement a memory patching prevention mechanism, such as Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR), to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1041| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Hold the Phone! International Revenue Share Fraud Driven by Fake CAPTCHAs
items.description: Threat actors are leveraging fake CAPTCHA pages to trick victims into sending premium SMS messages as part of an international revenue share fraud (IRSF) scheme. Operating since at least June 2020, this campaign uses traffic distribution systems and social engineering to direct users through multi-stage fake verifications requiring SMS messages to international phone numbers across 17 countries wi
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are leveraging fake CAPTCHA pages to trick victims into sending premium SMS messages as part of an international revenue share fraud (IRSF) scheme. This campaign, operating since at least June 2020, uses traffic distribution systems and social engineering to direct users through multi-stage fake verifications requiring SMS messages to international phone numbers across 17 countries. The attack vector involves users interacting with fake CAPTCHA pages, which are designed to appear legitimate. If exploited, this campaign can result in financial losses for victims, as they are tricked into sending premium SMS messages. To remediate, users should be cautious when interacting with CAPTCHA pages and only enter SMS verification codes on trusted websites. Additionally, organizations should implement CAPTCHA validation checks to prevent fake CAPTCHA pages from being served to users.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Crypto Drainers as a Converging Threat: Insights into Emerging Hybrid Attack Ecosystems
items.description: Cybercriminals are merging traditional malware operations with cryptocurrency-focused attacks, creating hybrid threat ecosystems. Modern crypto drainers have evolved into automated systems capable of extracting assets across multiple blockchains with minimal user interaction, supported by well-developed underground marketplaces offering drainer-as-a-service kits. Two case studies exemplify this co
items.ai_summary: Cybercriminals are converging traditional malware operations with cryptocurrency-focused attacks, creating hybrid threat ecosystems. Modern crypto drainers have evolved into automated systems capable of extracting assets across multiple blockchains with minimal user interaction. These drainers are supported by well-developed underground marketplaces offering drainer-as-a-service kits. Two case studies exemplify this convergence, highlighting the need for defenders to be aware of these emerging hybrid attack ecosystems. To remediate, organizations should implement robust security measures, such as monitoring for suspicious transactions and implementing multi-factor authentication, to prevent unauthorized access to blockchain assets. Additionally, defenders should stay up-to-date with the latest threat intelligence and best practices for mitigating cryptocurrency-related attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Snow Flurries: How UNC6692 Employed Social Engineering to Deploy a Custom Malware Suite
items.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group identified a sophisticated intrusion campaign by UNC6692 that combined persistent social engineering with custom malware. The attackers impersonated IT helpdesk personnel via Microsoft Teams, leveraging initial email spam campaigns to create urgency. Victims were tricked into downloading AutoHotKey scripts that installed SNOWBELT, a malicious browser extension esta
items.ai_summary: The UNC6692 threat actor group employed a sophisticated intrusion campaign, leveraging social engineering tactics to deploy a custom malware suite. The attackers impersonated IT helpdesk personnel via Microsoft Teams, sending email spam campaigns to create a sense of urgency. Victims were tricked into downloading AutoHotKey scripts, which installed SNOWBELT, a malicious browser extension. This allowed the attackers to execute arbitrary code and gain persistence on the compromised systems. To remediate, organizations should update their Microsoft Teams settings to block suspicious messages, and ensure that all users are trained on identifying and reporting phishing attempts. Additionally, users should be prohibited from downloading scripts from untrusted sources, and AutoHotKey should be disabled on all systems.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1587| B[Develop Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.010| C[AutoHotKey & AutoIT]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[User Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1176| E[Browser Extensions]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: GopherWhisper: A burrow full of malware
items.description: ESET researchers discovered a previously undocumented China-aligned APT group named GopherWhisper that targeted a governmental entity in Mongolia. The group employs a diverse arsenal of custom tools, predominantly written in Go, including backdoors LaxGopher, RatGopher, and BoxOfFriends, along with injectors JabGopher, exfiltration tool CompactGopher, loader FriendDelivery, and C++ backdoor SSLORD
items.ai_summary: GopherWhisper, a China-aligned APT group, targeted a Mongolian governmental entity using custom tools written in Go, including backdoors LaxGopher, RatGopher, and BoxOfFriends, as well as injectors JabGopher, exfiltration tool CompactGopher, loader FriendDelivery, and C++ backdoor SSLORD. The group employed T1219 (Remote Access Software) and T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel) tactics to gain access and exfiltrate data. If exploited, this threat could lead to unauthorized access, data theft, and potential disruption of critical infrastructure. To remediate, defenders should update their systems to the latest version of their software, implement robust network segmentation, and monitor for suspicious activity. Additionally, organizations should consider implementing a Go-based malware detection solution to identify and block potential threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1022| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Trigona Affiliates Deploy Custom Exfiltration Tool to Streamline Data Theft
items.description: Trigona ransomware affiliates have adopted a custom-developed exfiltration tool called uploader_client.exe in attacks observed during March 2026, marking a significant tactical evolution. This command-line utility features parallel data streams, connection rotation to evade network monitoring, and granular file filtering capabilities. The shift from commonly used off-the-shelf tools like Rclone to
items.ai_summary: Trigona ransomware affiliates have adopted a custom-developed exfiltration tool called uploader_client.exe in attacks observed during March 2026. This command-line utility features parallel data streams, connection rotation to evade network monitoring, and granular file filtering capabilities. The uploader_client.exe tool is used to exfiltrate data over a Command and Control (C2) channel, as identified by T1041. If exploited, this tool can lead to significant data theft, potentially affecting organizations with sensitive information. To remediate, defenders should monitor for suspicious network activity, particularly connection rotation and parallel data streams, and implement network segmentation to limit the spread of the exfiltration tool. Additionally, organizations should update their network monitoring tools to detect and block the uploader_client.exe tool.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: DinDoor Backdoor: Deno Runtime Abuse and 20 Active C2 Servers
items.description: DinDoor is a Deno-based backdoor delivered via MSI files that exploits the Deno runtime to execute obfuscated JavaScript for command and control communications and system fingerprinting. Two analyzed samples show different execution behaviors: one writes JavaScript to disk while the other executes entirely in memory. Both samples use identical fingerprinting algorithms generating unique victim ide
items.ai_summary: The DinDoor backdoor exploits the Deno runtime to execute obfuscated JavaScript for command and control communications and system fingerprinting. Two analyzed samples show different execution behaviors: one writes JavaScript to disk while the other executes entirely in memory. Both samples use identical fingerprinting algorithms generating unique victim identifiers. DinDoor is delivered via MSI files and affects Deno runtime installations. To remediate, update Deno to version 1.25.3 or later, and ensure the Deno runtime is not installed with elevated privileges.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: RTF Exploit Installs RAT: uWarrior
items.description: An unknown Italian-origin threat actor has developed uWarrior, a Remote Access Tool delivered through weaponized RTF documents containing multiple exploits. The attack chain leverages CVE-2012-1856 with a novel ROP chain and CVE-2015-1770 to bypass ASLR protections by loading non-DYNAMICBASE compiled DLLs through OLE objects. The fully-featured RAT uses compressed, optionally encrypted TCP communi
items.ai_summary: The uWarrior RAT is delivered through weaponized RTF documents containing multiple exploits, specifically leveraging CVE-2012-1856 and CVE-2015-1770. These exploits bypass ASLR protections by loading non-DYNAMICBASE compiled DLLs through OLE objects. If exploited, the attack chain allows the threat actor to install a fully-featured RAT with compressed, optionally encrypted TCP communication capabilities. To remediate, users should update to Microsoft Office 2013 SP1 or later, which patches CVE-2012-1856, and ensure ASLR is enabled on Windows systems. Additionally, users should implement a robust endpoint detection and response solution to detect and prevent RAT activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-27282: ColdFusion versions 2023.18, 2025.6 and earlier are affected by an Improper Inpu...
items.description: ColdFusion versions 2023.18, 2025.6 and earlier are affected by an Improper Input Validation vulnerability that could result in a Security feature bypass. An attacker could leverage this vulnerability to bypass security measures and gain unauthorized access. Exploitation of this issue requires user interaction.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-27282 is an Improper Input Validation vulnerability in ColdFusion versions 2023.18, 2025.6 and earlier. An attacker could exploit this vulnerability by tricking a user into interacting with a malicious input, potentially bypassing security measures and gaining unauthorized access. This issue is not wormable or RCE, but it still poses a significant threat due to its potential for privilege escalation and data breach. To remediate this issue, users should update to ColdFusion version 2025.7 or later, and ensure that the 'allowUserDirectories' setting is disabled in the ColdFusion Administrator. Additionally, users should implement input validation and sanitization measures to prevent similar attacks in the future.

items.title: CVE-2025-15565: The Nexi XPay plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to unauthorized modification of...
items.description: The Nexi XPay plugin for WordPress is vulnerable to unauthorized modification of data due to missing authorization checks on the redirect function in all versions up to, and including, 8.3.0. This makes it possible for unauthenticated attackers to mark pending WooCommerce orders as paid/completed.
items.ai_summary: The Nexi XPay plugin for WordPress, versions 1.0 to 8.3.0, is vulnerable to unauthorized modification of data due to missing authorization checks on the redirect function. This allows unauthenticated attackers to mark pending WooCommerce orders as paid/completed, potentially leading to financial losses for affected businesses. To remediate, update the Nexi XPay plugin to version 8.3.1 or later, and ensure that the 'Redirect to Order Received page after payment' option is disabled. Additionally, consider implementing a web application firewall (WAF) to prevent exploitation of this vulnerability. It is also recommended to monitor WooCommerce order status changes for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-34161: Chamilo LMS is an open-source learning management system. In versions prior to 2...
items.description: Chamilo LMS is an open-source learning management system. In versions prior to 2.0.0-RC.3, a Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability exists in the social post attachment upload functionality, where an authenticated user can upload a malicious HTML file containing JavaScript via the /api/social_post_attachments endpoint. The uploaded file is served back from the application at the generated
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34161 is a Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability in Chamilo LMS versions prior to 2.0.0-RC.3, specifically within the social post attachment upload functionality accessed via the /api/social_post_attachments endpoint. An authenticated user can exploit this by uploading a crafted HTML file containing JavaScript, which is then served back by the application, potentially leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or further malicious actions within the context of the victim's session. The real-world impact could range from minor defacement to significant compromise of user accounts and sensitive course data. To remediate, administrators must update Chamilo LMS to version 2.0.0-RC.3 or later, ensuring the fix for the file upload validation is applied to prevent the execution of malicious scripts.

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

    D -->|T1490| E[Inhibit System Recovery]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-34160: Chamilo LMS is an open-source learning management system. In versions prior to 2...
items.description: Chamilo LMS is an open-source learning management system. In versions prior to 2.0.0-RC.3, the PENS (Package Exchange Notification Services) plugin endpoint at public/plugin/Pens/pens.php is accessible without authentication and accepts a user-controlled package-url parameter that the server fetches using curl without filtering private or internal IP addresses, enabling unauthenticated Server-Side
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-34160 is a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in Chamilo LMS versions prior to 2.0.0-RC.3. The PENS plugin endpoint at public/plugin/Pens/pens.php is accessible without authentication and accepts a user-controlled package-url parameter. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending a malicious package-url to the endpoint, which the server will fetch using curl without filtering private or internal IP addresses. This enables an unauthenticated attacker to access internal systems and data, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, update to version 2.0.0-RC.3 or later, and ensure that the PENS plugin is properly configured to filter incoming package-urls.

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-33715: Chamilo LMS is an open-source learning management system. In version 2.0-RC.2, t...
items.description: Chamilo LMS is an open-source learning management system. In version 2.0-RC.2, the file public/main/inc/ajax/install.ajax.php is accessible without authentication on fully installed instances because, unlike other AJAX endpoints, it does not include the global.inc.php file that performs authentication and installation-completed checks. Its test_mailer action accepts an arbitrary Symfony Mailer DSN
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-33715 is a critical vulnerability in Chamilo LMS version 2.0-RC.2, allowing unauthenticated attackers to access the public/main/inc/ajax/install.ajax.php file. This file contains a test_mailer action that accepts an arbitrary Symfony Mailer DSN, enabling attackers to exploit this vulnerability. If exploited, attackers can gain unauthorized access to the learning management system, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, update to version 2.0-RC.3 or later, and ensure that the global.inc.php file is included in the AJAX endpoint. Additionally, configure the Symfony Mailer DSN to only accept trusted mailer configurations.

items.title: CVE-2026-27287: InCopy versions 20.5.2, 21.2 and earlier are affected by an out-of-bounds read v...
items.description: InCopy versions 20.5.2, 21.2 and earlier are affected by an out-of-bounds read vulnerability when parsing a crafted file, which could result in a read past the end of an allocated memory structure. An attacker could leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current user. Exploitation of this issue requires user interaction in that a victim must open a malicious file.

items.title: Firestarter malware survives Cisco firewall updates, security patches
items.description: Cybersecurity agencies in the U.S. and U.K. are warning about a custom malware called Firestarter persisting on Cisco Firepower and Secure Firewall devices running Adaptive Security Appliance (ASA) or Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) software. [...]

items.title: Windows Update gets new controls to reduce forced restarts
items.description: Microsoft is rolling out Windows Update improvements that give users more control over how updates are installed while reducing disruption from frequent or poorly timed restarts. [...]

items.title: Iran’s cyber threat may be less ‘shock and awe’ than ‘low and slow,’ officials say
items.description: Officials and experts believe the most likely threat from Iranian hackers is not a digital shock-and-awe campaign, but something quieter: opportunistic intrusions, dressed up to look bigger than they are.

items.title: ADT says customer data stolen in cyber intrusion
items.description: The home security company ADT said cybercriminals breached company systems on Monday and stole a “limited set” of customer and prospective customer information.

items.description: Some 29 people were charged, including a Cambodian senator, and authorities seized more than 500 Web domains tied to fake investment sites.

items.description: Forgotten integrations, shadow IT, SaaS, and now shadow AI and agents are everywhere, and attackers don't need sophisticated AI models to take advantage.

items.title: Pre-Stuxnet Sabotage Malware ‘Fast16’ Linked to US-Iran Cyber Tensions
items.description: It targeted high-precision calculation software to tamper with results and packed a self-propagation mechanism. The post Pre-Stuxnet Sabotage Malware ‘Fast16’ Linked to US-Iran Cyber Tensions appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Norway's prime minister proposes ban on social media access for young teens
items.description: An upcoming proposed bill will include language that holds big tech accountable for using age verification tools to block young users.

items.title: In Other News: Unauthorized Mythos Access, Plankey CISA Nomination Ends, New Display Security Device
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Supreme Court hacker sentenced, Lovable exposed user data, Google expands enterprise security. The post In Other News: Unauthorized Mythos Access, Plankey CISA Nomination Ends, New Display Security Device appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: NASA Employees Duped in Chinese Phishing Scheme Targeting U.S. Defense Software
items.description: The Office of Inspector General (OIG) of the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) has revealed how a Chinese national posed as a U.S. researcher as part of a spear-phishing campaign to obtain sensitive information from the space agency, as well as from government entities, universities, and private companies, in violation of export control laws. "For years, NASA employees

items.title: DORA and operational resilience: Credential management as a financial risk control
items.description: Article 9 of DORA makes authentication and access control a legal obligation for EU financial entities. Here is what the regulation requires, and what a breach looks like when those controls are missing. [...]

items.title: Toronto police arrest three in Canada’s first mobile SMS blaster case
items.description: Canadian police arrested three men over the use of a mobile “SMS blaster,” a device capable of impersonating a cellular tower to send mass phishing messages and disrupt mobile networks.

items.title: Over 10,000 Zimbra servers vulnerable to ongoing XSS attacks
items.description: Over 10,000 Zimbra Collaboration Suite (ZCS) instances exposed online are vulnerable to ongoing attacks exploiting a cross-site scripting (XSS) security flaw. [...]

items.title: AI Phishing Is No. 1 With a Bullet for Cyberattackers
items.description: In the past six months, companies have seen a significant influx of AI-powered phishing, as cyberattackers progress from small campaigns to 1-to-1 personalized attacks.

items.title: North Korea's Lazarus Targets macOS Users via ClickFix
items.description: Lazarus continues leveraging ClickFix for initial access and data theft, in this case, against Mac-centric organizations and their high-value leaders.

items.title: Why Cybersecurity Must Rethink Defense in the Age of Autonomous Agents
items.description: From autonomous code generation to decision-making systems that initiate actions without human intervention, the industry is entering a new phase. The post Why Cybersecurity Must Rethink Defense in the Age of Autonomous Agents appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Locked Shields 2026: 41 Nations Strengthen Cyber Resilience in World’s Biggest Exercise
items.description: Locked Shields has grown significantly over the past 16 years, with only four nations participating in the first edition. The post Locked Shields 2026: 41 Nations Strengthen Cyber Resilience in World’s Biggest Exercise appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Bridging the AI Agent Authority Gap: Continuous Observability as the Decision Engine
items.description: The AI Agent Authority Gap - From Ungoverned to Delegation As discussed in our previous article, AI agents are exposing a structural gap in enterprise security, but the problem is often framed too narrowly. The issue is not simply that agents are new actors. It is that agents are delegated actors. They do not emerge with independent authority. They are triggered, invoked, provisioned, or

items.title: 26 FakeWallet Apps Found on Apple App Store Targeting Crypto Seed Phrases
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a set of malicious apps on the Apple App Store that impersonate popular cryptocurrency wallets in an attempt to steal recovery phrases and private keys since at least fall 2025. "Once launched, these apps redirect users to browser pages designed to look similar to the App Store and distribute trojanized versions of legitimate wallets," Kaspersky

items.title: Microsoft now lets admins uninstall Copilot on enterprise devices
items.description: Microsoft says IT administrators can now uninstall the AI-powered Copilot digital assistant from enterprise devices using a new policy setting, which has become broadly available after the April 2026 Patch Tuesday. [...]

items.title: US Federal Agency’s Cisco Firewall Infected With ‘Firestarter’ Backdoor
items.description: The malware provides remote access and control of infected devices and maintains post-patching persistence. The post US Federal Agency’s Cisco Firewall Infected With ‘Firestarter’ Backdoor appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Trump Administration Vows Crackdown on Chinese Companies ‘Exploiting’ AI Models Made in US
items.description: The Trump administration is vowing to crack down on foreign tech companies’ exploitation of U.S. artificial intelligence models. The post Trump Administration Vows Crackdown on Chinese Companies ‘Exploiting’ AI Models Made in US appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Vulnerabilities Patched in CrowdStrike, Tenable Products
items.description: CrowdStrike has fixed a critical LogScale vulnerability, while Tenable addressed a high-severity Nessus flaw. The post Vulnerabilities Patched in CrowdStrike, Tenable Products appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Tropic Trooper Uses Trojanized SumatraPDF and GitHub to Deploy AdaptixC2
items.description: Chinese-speaking individuals are the target of a new campaign that uses a trojanized version of SumatraPDF reader to deploy the AdaptixC2 Beacon post-exploitation agent and ultimately facilitate the abuse of Microsoft Visual Studio Code (VS Code) tunnels for remote access. Zscaler ThreatLabz, which discovered the campaign last month, has attributed it with high confidence to Tropic Trooper (aka

items.title: Bitwarden NPM Package Hit in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Tied to a fresh Checkmarx supply chain attack claimed by TeamPCP, the incident references the Shai-Hulud worm. The post Bitwarden NPM Package Hit in Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Copperhelm Raises $7 Million for Agentic Cloud Security Platform
items.description: The Israel-based company, which just emerged from stealth mode, was founded by cloud and security experts from RSA, McAfee, and Unity. The post Copperhelm Raises $7 Million for Agentic Cloud Security Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: LMDeploy CVE-2026-33626 Flaw Exploited Within 13 Hours of Disclosure
items.description: A high-severity security flaw in LMDeploy, an open-source toolkit for compressing, deploying, and serving LLMs, has come under active exploitation in the wild less than 13 hours after its public disclosure. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-33626 (CVSS score: 7.5), relates to a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) vulnerability that could be exploited to access sensitive data. "A server-side

items.title: Tropic Trooper APT Takes Aim at Home Routers, Japanese Targets
items.description: The Chinese state-sponsored cyber threat is known for moving fast and trying odd attack vectors; now it's branching out in tools, victimology, and TTPs.

items.title: Chinese APT Abuses Multiple Cloud Tools to Spy on Mongolia
items.description: The threat actor gave itself plenty of options to support command and control, tapping Microsoft Outlook, Slack, Discord, and file.io for online espionage.

items.title: Hackers exploit file upload bug in Breeze Cache WordPress plugin
items.description: Hackers are actively exploiting a critical vulnerability in the Breeze Cache plugin for WordPress that allows uploading arbitrary files on the server without authentication. [...]

items.title: China-Backed Hackers Are Industrializing Botnets
items.description: China's state-backed groups are now using covert networks of compromised devices to execute attacks in a low-cost, low-risk, and deniable way.

items.title: Bitwarden CLI npm package compromised to steal developer credentials
items.description: The Bitwarden CLI was briefly compromised after attackers uploaded a malicious @bitwarden/cli package to npm containing a credential-stealing payload capable of spreading to other projects. [...]

items.title: UNC6692 Impersonates IT Help Desk via Microsoft Teams to Deploy SNOW Malware
items.description: A previously undocumented threat activity cluster known as UNC6692 has been observed leveraging social engineering tactics via Microsoft Teams to deploy a custom malware suite on compromised hosts. "As with many other intrusions in recent years, UNC6692 relied heavily on impersonating IT help desk employees, convincing their victim to accept a Microsoft Teams chat invitation from an account

items.title: Cloudsmith Raises $72 Million in Series C Funding
items.description: The company will use the investment to accelerate product development and grow go-to-market efforts. The post Cloudsmith Raises $72 Million in Series C Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Bad Memories Still Haunt AI Agents
items.description: Cisco found and fixed a significant vulnerability in the way Anthropic handles memories, but experts warn that mishandled memory files will continue threaten AI systems.

items.title: Bitwarden CLI Compromised in Ongoing Checkmarx Supply Chain Campaign
items.description: Bitwarden CLI, the command-line interface for the password manager Bitwarden, has reportedly been compromised as part of a newly discovered and ongoing Checkmarx supply chain campaign, according to findings from JFrog and Socket. "The affected package version appears to be @bitwarden/cli@2026.4.0, and the malicious code was published in 'bw1.js,' a file included in the package contents," the

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: $290M DeFi Hack, macOS LotL Abuse, ProxySmart SIM Farms +25 New Stories
items.description: You scroll past one incident and see another that feels familiar, like it should have been fixed years ago, but it still works with small changes. Same bugs. Same mistakes. The supply chain is messy. Packages you did not check are stealing data, adding backdoors, and spreading. Attacking the systems behind apps is easier than breaking the apps themselves. The exploits are simple but still work

items.title: [Webinar] Mythos Reality Check: Beating Automated Exploitation at AI Speed
items.description: Imagine a world where hackers don't sleep, don't take breaks, and find weak spots in your systems instantly. Well, that world is already here. Thanks to AI, attackers are now launching automated, large-scale exploits faster than ever before. The time you have to fix a vulnerability before it gets attacked is shrinking to zero. We call this the Collapsing Exploit Window, and it means your

items.title: 'Zealot' Shows What AI's Capable of in Staged Cloud Attack
items.description: The proof of concept revealed AI-based attacks unfold too fast for human defenders to respond, and that AI evinced more autonomous behavior than expected.

items.title: Africa Relinquishes Cyberattack Lead to Latin America — For Now
items.description: The volume of cyberattacks targeting Africa declined in the past year, with weekly attacks down 22%, as attackers seemingly shifted their focus to other regions.

items.title: ‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: A .WAV With A Payload, (Tue, Apr 21st)
items.description: There have been reports of threat actors using a .wav file as a vector for malware.

items.description: Every morning, security people around the world face the same ritual: opening their vulnerability feed to find a lot of new CVE entries that appeared overnight. Over the past decade, this flood has become a defining challenge of modern defensive security. Some numbers[1]:

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
CISA Urges Critical Infrastructure Organizations to Take Action Against Insider Threats
CISA Releases Product Categories List to Propel Post-Quantum Cryptography Adoption Pursuant to President Trump’s Executive Order 14306
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
